channel.title: James Miles' AI Coding Adventures
channel.description: Exploring the frontier of AI-assisted coding — experiments, insights, and adventures in building with AI.

item.title: Hello, World!
item.description: Welcome to James Miles' AI Coding Adventures — a blog about building software with AI assistants, one experiment at a time.

item.title: From the Archives: Enumerate This!
item.description: The articles below were migrated from Enumerate This!, my original C# and Reactive Extensions blog (2010-2016).

item.title: New Linq2Azure Release
item.description: We released a new version of Linq2Azure this week, allowing management of SQL Database Geo-Replicas.

item.title: YOW! West 2015
item.description: If anyone is interested in presenting at YOW! West this year, the submission page is now online.

item.title: LightBlue: An alternative to the Azure Emulator
item.description: LightBlue is a development framework that abstracts our dependency on the Azure platform, providing a light-weight hosting mechanism for worker and web roles.

item.title: Solving Puzzles in C#: Poker Hands
item.description: A brute force C# and LINQ solution to computing poker hands from a standard 52-card deck.

item.title: Record Types & Pattern-Matching Coming in C# 6?
item.description: A look at the draft specification for Records and Pattern-Matching in C#, borrowed from F# and Haskell.

item.title: Solving Puzzles with C#: Coins in a Bag
item.description: Solving a probability puzzle about biased coins using C# and LINQ in LINQPad.

item.title: Linq2Azure 1.1.0.0 released!
item.description: Linq2Azure 1.1.0.0 has been released.

item.title: Linq2Azure 1.0.0.7 released!
item.description: Linq2Azure 1.0.0.7 has been released.

item.title: Linq2Azure 1.0.0.1 – LINQPad driver update!
item.description: This update contains a bug fix for configuration serialization in the Linq2Azure LINQPad driver.

item.title: Linq2Azure – NuGet Package Released!
item.description: We've just released the Linq2Azure API to the NuGet Gallery for automation in applications and deployment scripts.

item.title: Asynchronous Commands in Metro, WPF & Silverlight
item.description: I've seen quite a few examples demonstrating the new async/await language features (C# 5 & VB next) with button click events;

item.title: Hosting your own poker tournament with LINQPad & Reactive Extensions
item.description: Playing poker next Saturday night & want to impress your friends with your programming skills?

item.title: Rxx 1.3 Released (Rx Contribution Project)
item.description: See; http://rxx.codeplex.com/

item.title: Reactive Extensions 2.0 Beta: Portable Libraries
item.description: Further to my last post about Rx 2.0 assemblies.

item.title: Reactive Extensions 2.0 Beta
item.description: Rx 2.0 is coming!

item.title: Reactive Extensions 2.0 Beta: Assembly References
item.description: Before you jump into Rx 2.0 Beta you should know about some of the changes to the hierarchy of assemblies.

item.title: Rx – Awaiting events & observables in C# 5
item.description: So everyone is now using C# 5 to write asynchronous methods to await tasks right 😉

item.title: Pop Quiz: Asynchronous Methods & Contract Errors
item.description: This is for people wanting to expand their asynchronous brains!

item.title: Reactive Extensions utilizing 64 cores
item.description: Saw this on Facebook… welcome to the future;

item.title: Hostile to friendly type names
item.description: Have you ever written some code that dumps type names using reflection and run into annoyances like this?

item.title: Structs that implement IEnumerator
item.description: OK. Lets just revise the query we had in the previous post. I’m going to change it so that is finds enumerators that are also value types;

item.title: Structs that implement IDisposable
item.description: A few weeks ago some friends and I were at the pub discussing value types that implement IDisposable. Consider the IObservable interface.

item.title: More Reactive Extensions Performance Improvements
item.description: I blogged about performance when Rx was officially released a few months ago.

item.title: Reactive Extensions Extensions 1.2
item.description: Full Release Notes Here!

item.title: Observable.Generate Pop Quiz
item.description: What would be the output of the following program;

item.title: Asus U36SD notebook running Windows 8 developer preview
item.description: I’ve just setup a new laptop running Windows 8 & Visual Studio 11 developer preview.

item.title: Financial Charts & Reactive Extensions
item.description: The price of a financial instrument is not a fixed value. It is something that changes over time, driven by market forces. It’s quite common to view this as a chart that is updated as the price cha...

item.title: New Rxx Release (It’s a big one!)
item.description: We couldn’t let Rx go live without an Rxx refresh and boy is it a big one! Dave Sexton & I are proud to announce Rxx 1.1. Firstly let me congratulate Dave. This is a major milestone for us and he h...

item.title: Nirvana 6 is out – now with Reactive Extensions
item.description: my-Channels Nirvana 6 has just been released:

item.title: Reactive Extensions: It’s Official & Interactive Extensions Returns!
item.description: Yay! Version 1.0 of Reactive Extensions has just been officially released!

item.title: Rx Official Release (more…)
item.description: Bunch of new videos and workshops.

item.title: Rx Performance Improvements
item.description: I think being a technology that enables us to easily write multithreaded, concurrent applications, a question often asked is;

item.title: Finding a game of Doom with Rx
item.description: Thanks to everyone for coming to our Rx presentation last night and thanks to Mitch for running the most isolated (in terms of distance) .NET community in the world! Towards the end of the presenta...

item.title: Rx Presentation – Perth Western Australia
item.description: Sorry for the lack of blog action. Over the past few weeks I’ve left London behind, been working in Hong Kong, and I’m currently holidaying in Australia.

item.title: Marble Diagram for MergeSubject<T>
item.description: Here is a marble diagram for my last post; Modelling Market Data Services in Rx with MergeSubject<T>

item.title: Modelling Market Data Services in Rx with MergeSubject<T>
item.description: Many banking middleware solutions allow a consumer (either another service in the bank or perhaps an external 3rd party) to subscribe to information about a financial instrument. There are numerous...

item.title: Modelling Market Data Services in Rx with Scan + Replay
item.description: In my last post I defined a new type of subject that is useful for modelling snapshot/update style Publish/Subscribe communications. An alternative to building your own subject is to create an enve...

item.title: PEX 4 FUN
item.description: Do you like coding & games? Check out PEX4FUN

item.description: http://tomasp.net/blog/formlets-in-linq.aspx – Love your work Tomas!

item.title: Nirvana .NET : Data Groups & Rx Clients
item.description: See my latest post on the my-Channels blog – Nirvana .NET : Data Groups & Rx Clients

item.title: Introducing MyChannels.Nirvana
item.description: Over the past three Months I’ve been working closely with the guys at my-Channels on a new .NET API for Nirvana. There was a general consensus that, being a Java port, the existing API was not a fr...

item.title: Nirvana 6 now supports Reactive Extensions!
item.description: my-Channels have just shipped an “early access release” of Nirvana 6.

item.title: Not bad… for a human
item.description: Just replaced the HDD in my Alienware M15x with a 256 GB Crucial C300.

item.title: Rx Schedulers – Reverse Trampoline
item.description: Recently I've been playing around with some *crazy* IScheduler implementations. I'm going to do a series of posts outlining some of my ideas. I've not had time to test or meditate on some of these ...

item.title: Does my cluster have quorum
item.description: I’m finally returning to my blog after a 2 month hiatus. In a previous series of posts, I was looking into various heart beating patterns in Rx. I generally don’t like “multi part blog series” as t...

item.title: C# 5.0 & Rx Schedulers
item.description: It’s been a pretty exciting week in the C# world. I initially had mixed feelings.

item.title: Monitoring cluster nodes in Rx
item.description: Ok. So we’ve now defined an observable query that tells us the connectivity of a heart beat / keep alive stream, based on some timeout interval.

item.title: Refining the heat beat timeout
item.description: I’ve been playing around with different ways of expressing the timeout described in my last post. To refresh your memory, I originally defined it like this;

item.title: Heart beats, keep alives & Rx
item.description: This one has come up 3 or 4 times this year, so I thought it was about time I blogged about it.

item.title: Latch design pattern in Rx
item.description: I just ran into some blog posts about the “latch design pattern”.

item.title: Latch design pattern in Rx – more….
item.description: My original example isn't quite true to the pattern. Here is an updated version.

item.title: Filtering by information in previous elements
item.description: I’ve been off work sick for the last 2 days so I thought I’d catch up on some blogging. It’s been a couple of weeks as I’ve been busy reading the Haskell language specification. There is obviously ...

item.title: Lazy<T> verses Memoize()
item.description: When we have a long running operation, a common optimisation is to cache the result and reuse it in subsequent requests. Given the ongoing shift to multithreaded applications it is important that t...

item.title: Function as Enumerable
item.description: I’ve found that converting a function into an enumerable (or an observable) can be a neat trick at times. Consider the following.

item.title: More ideas for enhanced language support
item.description: The Zip operator is useful for combining two or more lists of data, where by the elements are matched based on their index.

item.title: Language support for CombineLatest would be nice
item.description: CombineLatest is very useful for orchestrating parallel asynchronous/observable requests. For example;

item.title: “Warm” observables with Publish().RefCount()
item.description: So in the observable extensions class there is a nice little extension method that stands out from the rest. RefCount() is different from all the other kids in the class in that it’s for IConnecta...
